Optimal Transport for Machine Learners

Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Dual Problem

Duality turns the transport problem into a search for potentials rather than couplings. This chapter explains why potentials certify optimality, how ccc-transforms regularize them, and why the quadratic case reveals convex analysis behind Brenier maps. Linear-programming duality gives the discrete picture Bertsimas & Tsitsiklis, 1997, while the continuous form is one of the central theorems of optimal transport Villani, 2003Santambrogio, 2015.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Discrete Dual¶

The discrete dual gives finite-dimensional certificates of optimality. Its complementary-slackness conditions identify where an optimal coupling can put mass.

Definition: Admissible Potentials

For a cost matrix C∈Rn×mC\in\RR^{n\times m}C∈Rn×m and weights a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​, b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​, a pair (f,g)∈Rn×Rm(f,g)\in\RR^n\times\RR^m(f,g)∈Rn×Rm is admissible if it lies below the cost:

DC:={(f,g)∈Rn×Rm  :  fi+gj≤Cijfor all i,j}.\mathcal{D}_C \eqdef \left\{ (f,g)\in\RR^n\times\RR^m \;:\; f_i+g_j\le C_{ij} \quad\text{for all } i,j \right\}.DC​:={(f,g)∈Rn×Rm:fi​+gj​≤Cij​for all i,j}.
(1)

Equivalently, f⊕g≤Cf\oplus g\le Cf⊕g≤C entrywise.

The two vectors play the role of source and target prices; admissibility means that no transported pair is priced above its travel cost. The discrete Kantorovich problem is a linear program. Hence its value can be computed either by minimizing over couplings or by maximizing over a pair of dual vectors.

Proposition: Discrete Kantorovich Duality

For a cost matrix C∈Rn×mC\in\RR^{n\times m}C∈Rn×m and weights a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​, b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​,

LC(a,b)=max⁡(f,g)∈DC⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩.\mathcal{L}_C(a,b) = \max_{(f,g)\in\mathcal{D}_C} \langle f,a\rangle+\langle g,b\rangle.LC​(a,b)=(f,g)∈DC​max​⟨f,a⟩+⟨g,b⟩.
(2)
Proof

The Lagrangian for the primal problem

min⁡P≥0,  P1m=a,  P⊤1n=b⟨C,P⟩\min_{P\ge0,\;P\mathbf{1}_m=a,\;P^\top\mathbf{1}_n=b} \langle C,P\rangleP≥0,P1m​=a,P⊤1n​=bmin​⟨C,P⟩
(3)

is

min⁡P≥0max⁡f∈Rn,  g∈Rm⟨C,P⟩+⟨a−P1m,f⟩+⟨b−P⊤1n,g⟩.\min_{P\ge0}\max_{f\in\RR^n,\;g\in\RR^m} \langle C,P\rangle +\langle a-P\mathbf{1}_m,f\rangle +\langle b-P^\top\mathbf{1}_n,g\rangle .P≥0min​f∈Rn,g∈Rmmax​⟨C,P⟩+⟨a−P1m​,f⟩+⟨b−P⊤1n​,g⟩.
(4)

For a feasible linear program, strong duality allows one to exchange the minimum and maximum. The inner minimization becomes

min⁡P≥0⟨C−f1m⊤−1ng⊤,P⟩.\min_{P\ge0}\langle C-f\mathbf{1}_m^\top-\mathbf{1}_n g^\top,P\rangle.P≥0min​⟨C−f1m⊤​−1n​g⊤,P⟩.
(5)

This value is 0 when Cij−fi−gj≥0C_{ij}-f_i-g_j\ge0Cij​−fi​−gj​≥0 for all i,ji,ji,j, and is −∞-\infty−∞ otherwise. The remaining maximization is exactly (2).

The primal-dual optimality relation locates the support of an optimal plan. If PPP is optimal and (f,g)(f,g)(f,g) is optimal, then

supp⁡(P)⊂{(i,j):fi+gj=Cij}.\operatorname{supp}(P) \subset \left\{(i,j): f_i+g_j=C_{ij}\right\}.supp(P)⊂{(i,j):fi​+gj​=Cij​}.
(6)

Thus potentials are not transport maps themselves. They are certificates, and their equality set with the cost matrix is where an optimal coupling is allowed to place mass.

show_book_figure("dual-kantorovich-discrete-potentials")

Discrete Kantorovich dual potentials for the quadratic cost Cij=∣xi−yj∣2C_{ij}=|x_i-y_j|^2Cij​=∣xi​−yj​∣2. The upper strip shows the fixed source histogram in red and the target histogram in blue. The lower strip shows optimal dual vectors (f,g)(f,g)(f,g), with a gauge chosen so that ⟨f,a⟩=0\langle f,a\rangle=0⟨f,a⟩=0. Complementary slackness states that mass can be transported only through entries where fi+gj=Cijf_i+g_j=C_{ij}fi​+gj​=Cij​.

The interactive demo varies the target law and the number of bins. The lower curves are reconstructed from the active monotone transport graph, so the equality set moves as the coupling support changes.

Interactive panel. Use the geometry and slack controls to see feasible dual potentials touch the cost surface exactly on matched pairs.

The formula (2) also shows that (a,b)↦LC(a,b)(a,b)\mapsto\mathcal{L}_C(a,b)(a,b)↦LC​(a,b) is convex, being a supremum of linear functions. From the primal formulation, C↦LC(a,b)C\mapsto\mathcal{L}_C(a,b)C↦LC​(a,b) is concave.

Auction Algorithm and Dual Prices¶

Assignment algorithms become more transparent once one has dual variables. The auction algorithm is a dual-price method: it updates target prices and maintains an approximate complementary-slackness certificate. The tolerance ε\varepsilonε removes ties, stabilizes price updates, and gives a quantitative optimality certificate Bertsekas, 1981Bertsekas, 1992Mérigot & Thibert, 2020.

Consider the square assignment problem with costs CijC_{ij}Cij​ and rewrite it as a profit maximization problem with aij=−Cija_{ij}=-C_{ij}aij​=−Cij​. The auction algorithm keeps prices pjp_jpj​ on the target points and a partial assignment. For an unassigned source iii, define the best and second-best reduced profits

vi=max⁡j(aij−pj),ji∈arg max⁡j(aij−pj),wi=max⁡j≠ji(aij−pj).v_i=\max_j(a_{ij}-p_j), \qquad j_i\in\operatorname*{arg\,max}_j(a_{ij}-p_j), \qquad w_i=\max_{j\ne j_i}(a_{ij}-p_j).vi​=jmax​(aij​−pj​),ji​∈jargmax​(aij​−pj​),wi​=j=ji​max​(aij​−pj​).
(7)

Source iii bids for jij_iji​ and increases its price by the gap to the second-best target, plus a margin:

pji←pji+vi−wi+ε.p_{j_i}\leftarrow p_{j_i}+v_i-w_i+\varepsilon .pji​​←pji​​+vi​−wi​+ε.
(8)

The target jij_iji​ is assigned to iii, and its previous owner, if any, becomes unassigned. The iteration stops when all sources are assigned.

show_book_figure("dual-auction-progression")

Matrix view of actual auction iterates on a diagonally dominant one-dimensional squared-distance assignment. Each panel records the current ownership state: unassigned bidders are shown as flat rows, while assigned bidders are shown as one-hot rows at their currently held target. The snapshots show initialization, intermediate price updates, and the final identity assignment satisfying complementary slackness.

The interactive auction view exposes the price dynamics directly. Increasing ε\varepsilonε makes bids coarser and the final certificate looser; decreasing it makes the price landscape closer to exact complementary slackness.

Interactive panel. Use the step and gap controls to inspect how auction prices and assignments evolve toward complementary slackness.

For fixed prices ppp, eliminating the bidder utilities uiu_iui​ in the dual minimization gives the convex objective

D(p)=∑jpj+∑imax⁡j(aij−pj),D(p)=\sum_j p_j+\sum_i \max_j(a_{ij}-p_j),D(p)=j∑​pj​+i∑​jmax​(aij​−pj​),
(9)

which comes from the dual constraints ui+pj≥aiju_i+p_j\ge a_{ij}ui​+pj​≥aij​. The auction update should therefore be viewed as a price-adjustment method on this nonsmooth dual landscape, rather than as a generic gradient step.

Definition: ε\varepsilonε-Complementary Slackness

An assignment σ\sigmaσ and prices ppp satisfy ε\varepsilonε-complementary slackness if, for every source iii,

ai,σ(i)−pσ(i)≥max⁡j(aij−pj)−ε.a_{i,\sigma(i)}-p_{\sigma(i)} \ge \max_j(a_{ij}-p_j)-\varepsilon .ai,σ(i)​−pσ(i)​≥jmax​(aij​−pj​)−ε.
(10)
Proposition: Auction Optimality Certificate

If a complete assignment σ\sigmaσ satisfies ε\varepsilonε-complementary slackness, then it is nεn\varepsilonnε-optimal for the profit maximization problem, equivalently nεn\varepsilonnε-optimal for the original cost minimization problem. If all costs are integers and ε<1/n\varepsilon<1/nε<1/n, then σ\sigmaσ is optimal.

Proof

Let τ\tauτ be any assignment. By ε\varepsilonε-complementary slackness,

ai,τ(i)−pτ(i)≤max⁡j(aij−pj)≤ai,σ(i)−pσ(i)+ε.a_{i,\tau(i)}-p_{\tau(i)} \le \max_j(a_{ij}-p_j) \le a_{i,\sigma(i)}-p_{\sigma(i)}+\varepsilon .ai,τ(i)​−pτ(i)​≤jmax​(aij​−pj​)≤ai,σ(i)​−pσ(i)​+ε.
(11)

Summing over iii cancels the prices, since both σ\sigmaσ and τ\tauτ are permutations:

∑iai,τ(i)≤∑iai,σ(i)+nε.\sum_i a_{i,\tau(i)} \le \sum_i a_{i,\sigma(i)}+n\varepsilon .i∑​ai,τ(i)​≤i∑​ai,σ(i)​+nε.
(12)

No assignment has profit more than nεn\varepsilonnε above that of σ\sigmaσ. Since a=−Ca=-Ca=−C, the cost of σ\sigmaσ is at most nεn\varepsilonnε above the minimum cost. If the costs are integers, all assignment costs are integers; a gap strictly smaller than one forces the gap to be zero.

During the bidding process, the last bid made by a source makes its chosen target better, up to the margin ε\varepsilonε, than all alternatives. Subsequent price increases can only make targets less attractive, and one checks by induction that currently assigned pairs satisfy ε\varepsilonε-complementary slackness. A standard finite-termination proof normalizes prices by subtracting their minimum, observes that each bid increases one target price by at least ε\varepsilonε, and bounds the normalized price spread in terms of the range of the profits.

Remark: ϵ\epsilonϵ-scaling and relation with Sinkhorn

In practice one starts with a coarse ϵ\epsilonϵ and repeatedly decreases it, warm-starting the prices and assignment. This ϵ\epsilonϵ-scaling strategy is a homotopy method: large ϵ\epsilonϵ regularizes the combinatorial problem by enforcing a visible margin between the best and second-best reduced profits, while small ϵ\epsilonϵ recovers the exact dual certificate. If one wants a continuous-optimization analogy, the margin is closer to an exact-penalty or proximal continuation parameter than to a literal quadratic penalty.

Sinkhorn scaling plays a parallel role for entropic OT. There, the hard minimum in the dual ccc-transform is replaced by a soft minimum, or log-sum-exp, with temperature ϵ\epsilonϵ; in the auction algorithm, the hard maximum is kept but the complementary slackness condition is relaxed by ϵ\epsilonϵ. Both methods therefore use an ϵ\epsilonϵ-controlled dual continuation, and both recover the unregularized transport certificate as ϵ→0\epsilon\to0ϵ→0 under the usual assumptions. The outputs are different: Sinkhorn produces dense entropic couplings, whereas auction keeps a sparse assignment throughout.

Algorithm: Auction bidding with target prices

Input: Profit matrix A=(aij)A=(a_{ij})A=(aij​), bid increment ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0.

Output: Assignment map σ\sigmaσ.

Initialize: Set prices pj=0p_j=0pj​=0, ownership map o(j)=∅o(j)=\emptyseto(j)=∅, and U={1,…,n}U=\{1,\ldots,n\}U={1,…,n}.

While the unassigned set UUU is nonempty do:

Set i=min⁡Ui=\min Ui=minU.

Set ji=min⁡arg max⁡j(aij−pj)j_i=\min\argmax_j(a_{ij}-p_j)ji​=minargmaxj​(aij​−pj​).

Set vi=aiji−pjiv_i=a_{ij_i}-p_{j_i}vi​=aiji​​−pji​​ and wi=max⁡j≠ji(aij−pj)w_i=\max_{j\neq j_i}(a_{ij}-p_j)wi​=maxj=ji​​(aij​−pj​).

Update price: pji←pji+vi−wi+ϵ.p_{j_i}\leftarrow p_{j_i}+v_i-w_i+\epsilon.pji​​←pji​​+vi​−wi​+ϵ.

If o(ji)=i′≠∅o(j_i)=i'\neq\emptyseto(ji​)=i′=∅ then:

Set U←U∪{i′}U\leftarrow U\cup\{i'\}U←U∪{i′}.

Set o(ji)=io(j_i)=io(ji​)=i and U←U∖{i}U\leftarrow U\setminus\{i\}U←U∖{i}.

Return σ(i)=j\sigma(i)=jσ(i)=j iff o(j)=io(j)=io(j)=i.

General Formulation¶

The continuous dual is the analytic counterpart of the discrete linear program. It uses continuous potentials because measures are naturally probed through integration. The pairing is ⟨f,α⟩:=∫f  dα\langle f,\alpha\rangle\eqdef\int f\,\d\alpha⟨f,α⟩:=∫fdα.

Proposition: Kantorovich Duality

Assume that X\XX and Y\YY are compact metric spaces and that c∈C(X×Y)c\in\Cc(\X\times\Y)c∈C(X×Y). Then

Lc(α,β)=max⁡(f,g)∈Dc∫Xf(x)  dα(x)+∫Yg(y)  dβ(y),\mathcal{L}_c(\alpha,\beta) = \max_{(f,g)\in\mathcal{D}_c} \int_\X f(x)\,\d\alpha(x) + \int_\Y g(y)\,\d\beta(y),Lc​(α,β)=(f,g)∈Dc​max​∫X​f(x)dα(x)+∫Y​g(y)dβ(y),
(13)

where

Dc:={(f,g)∈C(X)×C(Y)  :  f(x)+g(y)≤c(x,y)for all (x,y)}.\mathcal{D}_c \eqdef \left\{ (f,g)\in\Cc(\X)\times\Cc(\Y) \;:\; f(x)+g(y)\le c(x,y) \quad\text{for all }(x,y) \right\}.Dc​:={(f,g)∈C(X)×C(Y):f(x)+g(y)≤c(x,y)for all (x,y)}.
(14)

The same formula extends under the usual lower-semicontinuity and integrability assumptions, replacing maxima by suprema when dual optimizers need not exist.

Proof

Weak duality is immediate. If f(x)+g(y)≤c(x,y)f(x)+g(y)\le c(x,y)f(x)+g(y)≤c(x,y) and π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)π∈Π(α,β), then

∫f  dα+∫g  dβ=∫(f(x)+g(y))  dπ(x,y)≤∫c(x,y)  dπ(x,y).\int f\,\d\alpha+\int g\,\d\beta = \int (f(x)+g(y))\,\d\pi(x,y) \le \int c(x,y)\,\d\pi(x,y).∫fdα+∫gdβ=∫(f(x)+g(y))dπ(x,y)≤∫c(x,y)dπ(x,y).
(15)

Taking the supremum over admissible potentials and the infimum over couplings gives the inequality ``≤\le≤’'.

For the reverse inequality, view the primal problem as a linear program over Radon measures, paired with C(X×Y)\Cc(\X\times\Y)C(X×Y). The affine marginal map is continuous for the weak topology, the feasible set is nonempty because it contains α⊗β\alpha\otimes\betaα⊗β, and the cost is continuous and bounded on compact sets. Since probability measures on a compact product are weakly compact, the set of attainable cost-marginal triples is closed after adding an epigraph variable. A separating-hyperplane argument applied to the convex set

{(π1,π2,∫c  dπ+r):π≥0, r≥0}\left\{ \left(\pi_1,\pi_2,\int c\,\d\pi+r\right) : \pi\ge0,\ r\ge0 \right\}{(π1​,π2​,∫cdπ+r):π≥0, r≥0}
(16)

gives continuous functions (f,g)(f,g)(f,g) and a scalar multiplier, normalized so that f⊕g≤cf\oplus g\le cf⊕g≤c. The separating inequality states that the supremum over such potentials is at least the primal value. This is the infinite-dimensional analogue of the finite linear-programming proof.

Remark: Dual attainment from ccc-transforms

Under the compactness and continuity assumptions of Proposition Proposition: Kantorovich Duality, the maximum in (13) is attained. If ccc is Lipschitz, Proposition Proposition: Lipschitz Stability Of ccc-Transforms shows that one may replace an admissible pair by its ccc-transforms without decreasing the dual objective; after fixing one additive gauge, the transformed potentials are uniformly bounded and equi-Lipschitz. Arzel`a--Ascoli then gives a converging maximizing subsequence, and the closed constraint f⊕g≤cf\oplus g\leq cf⊕g≤c passes to the limit.

The discrete case corresponds to dual vectors that are samples of continuous potentials, (fi,gj)=(f(xi),g(yj))(f_i,g_j)=(f(x_i),g(y_j))(fi​,gj​)=(f(xi​),g(yj​)). The primal-dual support condition becomes

supp⁡(π)⊂{(x,y)∈X×Y:f(x)+g(y)=c(x,y)}.\operatorname{supp}(\pi) \subset \left\{ (x,y)\in\X\times\Y : f(x)+g(y)=c(x,y) \right\}.supp(π)⊂{(x,y)∈X×Y:f(x)+g(y)=c(x,y)}.
(17)

For the one-dimensional quadratic cost, the continuous potentials can be read from the monotone map T=Fβ−1∘FαT=F_\beta^{-1}\circ F_\alphaT=Fβ−1​∘Fα​: on the active graph, f′(x)=2(x−T(x))f'(x)=2(x-T(x))f′(x)=2(x−T(x)) and g=fcg=f^cg=fc.

show_book_figure("dual-kantorovich-continuous-potentials")

Continuous Kantorovich potentials for the same source and target families as the discrete potential figure. The upper strips show the source density α\alphaα in red and the target density β\betaβ in blue. The lower strips show potentials fff and g=fcg=f^cg=fc for the quadratic cost c(x,y)=∣x−y∣2c(x,y)=|x-y|^2c(x,y)=∣x−y∣2. The equality set f(x)+g(y)=c(x,y)f(x)+g(y)=c(x,y)f(x)+g(y)=c(x,y) contains the monotone transport graph.

The same optimality condition is even more explicit if one plots the slack

s(x,y)=c(x,y)−f(x)−g(y).s(x,y)=c(x,y)-f(x)-g(y).s(x,y)=c(x,y)−f(x)−g(y).
(18)

Dual feasibility is the statement s≥0s\ge 0s≥0, while complementary slackness says that an optimal coupling can only live where this slack vanishes. The next figure shows this zero set for a smooth one-dimensional mixture example: the transported graph is not guessed from the potentials separately, but appears as the contact valley of the dual inequality.

show_book_figure("dual-complementary-slackness-contacts", width=620)

Complementary slackness contacts for one-dimensional quadratic OT between two Gaussian-mixture densities. The central heatmap displays the nonnegative dual slack s(x,y)=∣x−y∣2−f(x)−g(y)s(x,y)=|x-y|^2-f(x)-g(y)s(x,y)=∣x−y∣2−f(x)−g(y): pale colors mark the contact valley and darker blue means larger strict slack. The red top strip and blue side strip show the source and target densities. The violet curve and dots are the quantile transport contacts (x,T(x))(x,T(x))(x,T(x)), where s(x,T(x))=0s(x,T(x))=0s(x,T(x))=0 and the optimal Monge plan is supported.

The interactive view computes the monotone map from numerical quantiles and then integrates f′(x)=2(x−T(x))f'(x)=2(x-T(x))f′(x)=2(x−T(x)). It makes clear that the potentials change smoothly with the target law, even though the equality set remains a thin transport graph.

Interactive panel. Use the regularity and time controls to view continuous Kantorovich potentials and their active transport contacts.

In contrast to the primal problem, showing existence of solutions to the continuous dual is nontrivial: the constraint set is not compact and the objective is not coercive. The machinery of ccc-transforms repairs this by replacing arbitrary potentials with regular best responses.

Algorithm: Hard alternating ccc-transform closure

Input: Source potential fff on X\XX, cost ccc.

Output: Closed ccc-concave pair (f~,g~)(\tilde f,\tilde g)(f~​,g~​).

Set target best response: g=fc,g(y)=inf⁡x∈X c(x,y)−f(x).g=f^c, \qquad g(y)=\inf_{x\in\X}\ c(x,y)-f(x).g=fc,g(y)=infx∈X​ c(x,y)−f(x).

Set source closure: f~=gcˉ=fccˉ.\tilde f=g^{\bar c}=f^{c\bar c}.f~​=gcˉ=fccˉ.

Set closed target potential: g~=f~c=fccˉc=fc.\tilde g=\tilde f^c=f^{c\bar c c}=f^c.g~​=f~​c=fccˉc=fc. Return (f~,g~)=(fccˉ,fc)(\tilde f,\tilde g)=(f^{c\bar c},f^c)(f~​,g~​)=(fccˉ,fc).

c-Transforms¶

The ccc-transform is the operation that improves potentials without changing feasibility. It is both a proof device for dual attainment and the route from duality to Brenier’s convex potentials.

Best-Response Potentials¶

Keeping a dual potential fff fixed, one can maximize in closed form over the second potential in the dual problem:

sup⁡g{∫g  dβ  :  g(y)≤c(x,y)−f(x)for all x,y}.\sup_g \left\{ \int g\,\d\beta \;:\; g(y)\le c(x,y)-f(x) \quad\text{for all }x,y \right\}.gsup​{∫gdβ:g(y)≤c(x,y)−f(x)for all x,y}.
(19)

The constraint is equivalent to g(y)≤fc(y)g(y)\le f^c(y)g(y)≤fc(y).

Definition: ccc-Transform

For a function f:X→R∪{−∞}f:\X\to\RR\cup\{-\infty\}f:X→R∪{−∞}, its ccc-transform is

fc(y):=inf⁡x∈Xc(x,y)−f(x).f^c(y) \eqdef \inf_{x\in\X} c(x,y)-f(x).fc(y):=x∈Xinf​c(x,y)−f(x).
(20)

For a function g:Y→R∪{−∞}g:\Y\to\RR\cup\{-\infty\}g:Y→R∪{−∞}, the cˉ\bar ccˉ-transform associated with cˉ(y,x)=c(x,y)\bar c(y,x)=c(x,y)cˉ(y,x)=c(x,y) is

gcˉ(x):=inf⁡y∈Yc(x,y)−g(y).g^{\bar c}(x) \eqdef \inf_{y\in\Y} c(x,y)-g(y).gcˉ(x):=y∈Yinf​c(x,y)−g(y).
(21)
Remark: Discrete ccc-transform

If α=∑i=1naiδxi\al=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\de_{x_i}α=∑i=1n​ai​δxi​​ and β=∑j=1mbjδyj\be=\sum_{j=1}^m b_j\de_{y_j}β=∑j=1m​bj​δyj​​, the same definition applies to the finite spaces X={xi}i\X=\{x_i\}_iX={xi​}i​ and Y={yj}j\Y=\{y_j\}_jY={yj​}j​. The dual potentials are then vectors (f,g)∈Rn×Rm(\fD,\gD)\in\RR^n\times\RR^m(f,g)∈Rn×Rm, as in Section Discrete Dual, and the cost is the matrix C∈Rn×m\C\in\RR^{n\times m}C∈Rn×m with entries

Ci,j=c(xi,yj).\C_{i,j}=c(x_i,y_j).Ci,j​=c(xi​,yj​).
(22)

With the present sign convention f⊕g≤cf\oplus g\leq cf⊕g≤c, the discrete transform of f\fDf is the vector fC∈Rm\fD^\C\in\RR^mfC∈Rm defined by

(fC)j=min⁡1≤i≤nCi,j−fi.(\fD^\C)_j = \min_{1\leq i\leq n} \C_{i,j}-\fD_i.(fC)j​=1≤i≤nmin​Ci,j​−fi​.
(23)

This is a minimum because feasibility imposes the upper bound gj≤Ci,j−fi\gD_j\leq \C_{i,j}-\fD_igj​≤Ci,j​−fi​ for every iii. Thus the largest feasible best response is the minimum of these upper bounds; formulas with a maximum correspond to a different sign convention, not to the dual constraint (2). With Cˉ:=C⊤\bar\C\eqdef\C^\topCˉ:=C⊤, the opposite transform of g\gDg is

gCˉ:=gC⊤∈Rn,(gCˉ)i=min⁡1≤j≤mCi,j−gj.\gD^{\bar\C}\eqdef \gD^{\C^\top}\in\RR^n, \qquad (\gD^{\bar\C})_i = \min_{1\leq j\leq m} \C_{i,j}-\gD_j.gCˉ:=gC⊤∈Rn,(gCˉ)i​=1≤j≤mmin​Ci,j​−gj​.
(24)

Thus the continuous best-response operation reduces exactly to taking column minima for fC\fD^\CfC, or row minima for gCˉ\gD^{\bar\C}gCˉ, in shifted cost matrices. Chapter Paragraph studies the hybrid case where α\alα has a density and β\beβ is discrete; then gcˉ(x)=min⁡jc(x,yj)−gjg^{\bar c}(x)=\min_j c(x,y_j)-g_jgcˉ(x)=minj​c(x,yj​)−gj​ is a lower envelope over target atoms, and the regions where each atom realizes the minimum are the Laguerre cells for the quadratic cost.

Since β\betaβ is positive, maximizing ∫g  dβ\int g\,\d\beta∫gdβ is achieved by taking g=fcg=f^cg=fc on the support of β\betaβ, equivalently β\betaβ-almost everywhere.

show_book_figure("dual-c-transform-envelope")

Discrete ccc-transform as a lower envelope for costs cp(x,y)=∣x−y∣pc_p(x,y)=|x-y|^pcp​(x,y)=∣x−y∣p. The red circles are four source atoms xix_ixi​ with potential values fif_ifi​; the gray curves are y↦cp(xi,y)−fiy\mapsto c_p(x_i,y)-f_iy↦cp​(xi​,y)−fi​; the colored curve is their lower envelope fc(y)=min⁡icp(xi,y)−fif^c(y)=\min_i c_p(x_i,y)-f_ifc(y)=mini​cp​(xi​,y)−fi​. This is the semi-discrete situation where the source space is finite.

The interactive envelope view exposes the exponent, the number of atoms, and the potential amplitude. This is the local mechanism behind many dual regularity statements: taking a pointwise minimum of translated costs inherits regularity from the cost.

Interactive panel. Use the support and curvature controls to see the c-transform as a lower envelope of shifted cost functions.

Proposition: ccc-Transforms Solve The Semi-Relaxed Problems

For fixed fff, the maximizers of the dual objective over all ggg such that f⊕g≤cf\oplus g\le cf⊕g≤c are exactly the functions satisfying g=fcg=f^cg=fc β\betaβ-almost everywhere. Equivalently,

inf⁡π: π2=β∫c(x,y)  dπ(x,y)−∫f(x)  dπ1(x)=∫fc(y)  dβ(y).\inf_{\pi:\,\pi_2=\beta} \int c(x,y)\,\d\pi(x,y)-\int f(x)\,\d\pi_1(x) = \int f^c(y)\,\d\beta(y).π:π2​=βinf​∫c(x,y)dπ(x,y)−∫f(x)dπ1​(x)=∫fc(y)dβ(y).
(25)

Symmetrically, for fixed ggg, the maximizers over fff satisfy f=gcˉf=g^{\bar c}f=gcˉ α\alphaα-almost everywhere.

Proof

The constraint f(x)+g(y)≤c(x,y)f(x)+g(y)\le c(x,y)f(x)+g(y)≤c(x,y) for all xxx is equivalent, for each fixed yyy, to

g(y)≤inf⁡xc(x,y)−f(x)=fc(y).g(y)\le \inf_x c(x,y)-f(x)=f^c(y).g(y)≤xinf​c(x,y)−f(x)=fc(y).
(26)

Since β\betaβ is nonnegative, the largest possible value of ∫g  dβ\int g\,\d\beta∫gdβ is obtained by saturating this pointwise upper bound on the support of β\betaβ. The proof for f=gcˉf=g^{\bar c}f=gcˉ is identical after exchanging the two marginals.

For the primal formula, disintegrate any feasible plan as π( dx, dy)=πy( dx)β( dy)\pi(\d x,\d y)=\pi_y(\d x)\beta(\d y)π(dx,dy)=πy​(dx)β(dy). Then

∫c  dπ−∫f  dπ1=∫(∫(c(x,y)−f(x))  dπy(x)) dβ(y)≥∫fc(y)  dβ(y).\int c\,\d\pi-\int f\,\d\pi_1 = \int \left( \int (c(x,y)-f(x))\,\d\pi_y(x) \right)\d\beta(y) \ge \int f^c(y)\,\d\beta(y).∫cdπ−∫fdπ1​=∫(∫(c(x,y)−f(x))dπy​(x))dβ(y)≥∫fc(y)dβ(y).
(27)

Equality is obtained by choosing conditional laws supported on minimizers of x↦c(x,y)−f(x)x\mapsto c(x,y)-f(x)x↦c(x,y)−f(x), or by approximate measurable selections when exact selections are unavailable.

The map (f,g)↦(gcˉ,fc)(f,g)\mapsto(g^{\bar c},f^c)(f,g)↦(gcˉ,fc) replaces dual potentials by better ones: it preserves feasibility and improves the dual objective. Functions of the form fcf^cfc and gcˉg^{\bar c}gcˉ are called ccc-concave and cˉ\bar ccˉ-concave functions. These partial minimizations define maximizers on the supports of α\alphaα and β\betaβ, while the transform itself defines functions on the whole ambient spaces. This gives a canonical extension of dual solutions beyond their active supports.

Proposition: Lipschitz Stability Of ccc-Transforms

If ccc is LLL-Lipschitz with respect to its second variable, uniformly in the first one and for the metric dYd_\YdY​ on Y\YY, then fcf^cfc is LLL-Lipschitz.

Proof

For each xxx, set Fx(y)=c(x,y)−f(x)F_x(y)=c(x,y)-f(x)Fx​(y)=c(x,y)−f(x) and F(y)=fc(y)=inf⁡xFx(y)F(y)=f^c(y)=\inf_x F_x(y)F(y)=fc(y)=infx​Fx​(y). Since all functions FxF_xFx​ are LLL-Lipschitz,

∣F(y)−F(y′)∣=∣inf⁡xFx(y)−inf⁡xFx(y′)∣≤sup⁡x∣Fx(y)−Fx(y′)∣≤LdY(y,y′).|F(y)-F(y')| = \left|\inf_x F_x(y)-\inf_x F_x(y')\right| \le \sup_x |F_x(y)-F_x(y')| \le L d_\Y(y,y').∣F(y)−F(y′)∣=∣∣​xinf​Fx​(y)−xinf​Fx​(y′)∣∣​≤xsup​∣Fx​(y)−Fx​(y′)∣≤LdY​(y,y′).
(28)

This stability is crucial for dual attainment. When ccc is Lipschitz on compact spaces, one can replace arbitrary admissible potentials by ccc-transformed ones with a uniform Lipschitz bound; after fixing the harmless additive gauge, compactness follows from Arzela--Ascoli.

Euclidean Case¶

The Euclidean quadratic cost is the model case where ccc-transforms become ordinary convex conjugates after removing quadratic terms. This is the algebraic bridge between Kantorovich duality and Brenier maps.

The cost c(x,y)=−⟨x,y⟩c(x,y)=-\langle x,y\ranglec(x,y)=−⟨x,y⟩ on X=Y=Rd\X=\Y=\RR^dX=Y=Rd is central because it reduces the quadratic Wasserstein problem to convex duality. Indeed, for any π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)π∈Π(α,β),

∫∥x−y∥2  dπ(x,y)=∫∥x∥2  dα(x)+∫∥y∥2  dβ(y)−2∫⟨x,y⟩  dπ(x,y).\int \norm{x-y}^2\,\d\pi(x,y) = \int\norm{x}^2\,\d\alpha(x) + \int\norm{y}^2\,\d\beta(y) -2\int \langle x,y\rangle\,\d\pi(x,y).∫∥x−y∥2dπ(x,y)=∫∥x∥2dα(x)+∫∥y∥2dβ(y)−2∫⟨x,y⟩dπ(x,y).
(29)

For c(x,y)=−⟨x,y⟩c(x,y)=-\langle x,y\ranglec(x,y)=−⟨x,y⟩,

fc(y)=inf⁡x−⟨x,y⟩−f(x)=−(−f)∗(y),h∗(y):=sup⁡x⟨x,y⟩−h(x).f^c(y) = \inf_x -\langle x,y\rangle-f(x) = -(-f)^*(y), \qquad h^*(y)\eqdef\sup_x \langle x,y\rangle-h(x).fc(y)=xinf​−⟨x,y⟩−f(x)=−(−f)∗(y),h∗(y):=xsup​⟨x,y⟩−h(x).
(30)

Thus ccc-concave functions are negatives of convex functions. In the one-dimensional bilinear model case, the hard double ccc-transform is an operation of taking concave envelopes.

Remark: Proof of Brenier’s theorem

For c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\norm{x-y}^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2, subtracting the harmless quadratic terms reduces the geometry to the bilinear cost −⟨x,y⟩-\dotp{x}{y}−⟨x,y⟩. The primal-dual relationship, together with the fact that one can replace (f,g)(f,g)(f,g) by (fccˉ,fc)(f^{c\bar c},f^c)(fccˉ,fc), shows that an optimal plan satisfies

supp⁡(π)⊂{(x,y)  :  ϕ(x)+ϕ∗(y)=⟨x,y⟩},\supp(\pi) \subset \enscond{(x,y)}{\phi(x)+\phi^*(y)=\dotp{x}{y}},supp(π)⊂{(x,y):ϕ(x)+ϕ∗(y)=⟨x,y⟩},
(31)

where ϕ=−fccˉ\phi=-f^{c\bar c}ϕ=−fccˉ is convex and −g=ϕ∗-g=\phi^*−g=ϕ∗. By the Fenchel inequality, equality holds exactly when y∈∂ϕ(x)y\in\partial\phi(x)y∈∂ϕ(x). If α\alα has a density, convex functions are differentiable Lebesgue-almost everywhere, hence α\alα-almost everywhere, so ∂ϕ(x)\partial\phi(x)∂ϕ(x) is a singleton for α\alα-almost every xxx. This yields the Brenier map T=∇ϕT=\nabla\phiT=∇ϕ and explains why the optimal coupling is concentrated on a graph.

Failure of Alternating Optimization¶

A crucial property of the Legendre transform is that f∗∗∗=f∗f^{***}=f^*f∗∗∗=f∗, and that f∗∗f^{**}f∗∗ is the convex envelope of fff. These properties carry over to ccc-transforms Rockafellar, 2015.

Proposition: Algebra Of ccc-Transforms

The following identities hold, with inequalities understood pointwise:

(i) f≤f′⇒fc≥f′c,(ii) fccˉ≥f,\text{(i) } f\le f' \Rightarrow f^c\ge f'^c, \qquad \text{(ii) } f^{c\bar c}\ge f,(i) f≤f′⇒fc≥f′c,(ii) fccˉ≥f,
(32)
(iii) gcˉc≥g,(iv) fccˉc=fc.\text{(iii) } g^{\bar c c}\ge g, \qquad \text{(iv) } f^{c\bar c c}=f^c.(iii) gcˉc≥g,(iv) fccˉc=fc.
(33)
Proof

The first inequality follows directly from the definition because of the minus sign. For (ii),

fccˉ(x)=min⁡yc(x,y)−fc(y)=min⁡yc(x,y)−min⁡x′(c(x′,y)−f(x′)).f^{c\bar c}(x) = \min_y c(x,y)-f^c(y) = \min_y c(x,y)-\min_{x'}\left(c(x',y)-f(x')\right).fccˉ(x)=ymin​c(x,y)−fc(y)=ymin​c(x,y)−x′min​(c(x′,y)−f(x′)).
(34)

Since −min⁡x′(c(x′,y)−f(x′))≥−(c(x,y)−f(x))-\min_{x'}(c(x',y)-f(x'))\ge -(c(x,y)-f(x))−minx′​(c(x′,y)−f(x′))≥−(c(x,y)−f(x)), one obtains

fccˉ(x)≥min⁡yc(x,y)−c(x,y)+f(x)=f(x).f^{c\bar c}(x) \ge \min_y c(x,y)-c(x,y)+f(x) = f(x).fccˉ(x)≥ymin​c(x,y)−c(x,y)+f(x)=f(x).
(35)

The relation gcˉc≥gg^{\bar c c}\ge ggcˉc≥g is symmetric. To prove (iv), apply (ii) and then (i) with f′=fccˉf'=f^{c\bar c}f′=fccˉ to get fc≥fccˉcf^c\ge f^{c\bar c c}fc≥fccˉc. Applying (iii) to g=fcg=f^cg=fc gives the reverse inequality.

This invariance property shows that one can improve only once by exact alternating maximization:

(f,g)↦(f,fc)↦(fccˉ,fc)↦(fccˉ,fccˉc)=(fccˉ,fc).(f,g)\mapsto(f,f^c) \mapsto(f^{c\bar c},f^c) \mapsto(f^{c\bar c},f^{c\bar c c}) =(f^{c\bar c},f^c).(f,g)↦(f,fc)↦(fccˉ,fc)↦(fccˉ,fccˉc)=(fccˉ,fc).
(36)

Thus the method reaches a stationary point immediately. This is the classical failure mode of alternating maximization on a nonsmooth problem, where the nonsmooth constraint mixes the two variables. The workaround is to introduce smoothing, which the next chapters develop through entropic regularization and Sinkhorn scaling.

For the bilinear cost c(x,y)=−xyc(x,y)=-xyc(x,y)=−xy on a compact interval, ccc-concave functions are ordinary concave functions and fccˉf^{c\bar c}fccˉ is the smallest concave majorant of fff. A hard transform removes non-concave oscillations in one closure step instead of producing a gradual ascent.

show_book_figure("dual-alternating-c-transform-failure")

Hard ccc-transforms for the bilinear cost c(x,y)=−xyc(x,y)=-xyc(x,y)=−xy. Dark curves are the double-transform closures fccˉf^{c\bar c}fccˉ and gcˉcg^{\bar c c}gcˉc, which are concave majorants, while dashed lighter curves are the one-sided best responses gcˉg^{\bar c}gcˉ and fcf^cfc after a harmless vertical gauge shift. Exact alternating best responses are useful for certificates but do not give the smooth iterative dynamics later provided by entropic regularization.

The final interactive demo turns this algebra into a visible operation: change the roughness of the starting potential and observe that the hard double transform jumps directly to its concave closure.

Interactive panel. Use the iteration and asymmetry controls to see why alternating c-transforms can stall or fail without the right assumptions.

References¶
  1. Bertsimas, D., & Tsitsiklis, J. N. (1997). Introduction to Linear Optimization. Athena Scientific.
  2. Villani, C. (2003). Topics in Optimal Transportation (Vol. 58). American Mathematical Society.
  3. Santambrogio, F. (2015). Optimal Transport for Applied Mathematicians: Calculus of Variations, PDEs, and Modeling. Birkhäuser.
  4. Bertsekas, D. P. (1981). A new algorithm for the assignment problem. Mathematical Programming, 21(1), 152–171.
  5. Bertsekas, D. P. (1992). Auction algorithms for network flow problems: a tutorial introduction. Computational Optimization and Applications, 1(1), 7–66.
  6. Mérigot, Q., & Thibert, B. (2020). Optimal transport: discretization and algorithms. arXiv Preprint arXiv:2003.00855.
  7. Rockafellar, R. T. (2015). Convex Analysis. Princeton university press.
